title: Early Tick Season Raises Concerns Among Doctors
summary: Tick season in the United States appears to be starting earlier and more intensely than usual, with a higher number of bites already reported. This has led some doctors to worry about a potentially severe year for tick-borne diseases, such as Lyme disease. Dr. Alina Filozov, an infectious disease specialist in Connecticut, notes that increased exposure to ticks likely means more infections. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) issued an early advisory urging the public to take precautions. According to CDC Lyme disease expert Alison Hinckley, data indicates that ticks are active and people are being bitten now, even though tick bites typically peak in May. The CDC's tracking system shows that weekly ER visits for tick bites are at their highest for this time of year since 2017, across all regions except the south-central U.S. However, the data is limited because it only includes hospital visits, not all bites. Researchers will need months to systematically sample tick populations, and it will take time to determine if there is an actual surge in tick-borne illnesses. Not every tick bite leads to infection, but the early signs are concerning.

the time when something is at its highest level

background_read: Ticks are arachnids that feed on blood. They can transmit pathogens that cause diseases like Lyme disease, anaplasmosis, and babesiosis. The blacklegged tick (deer tick) is a common carrier of Lyme disease in the U.S. Preventative measures include using insect repellent, wearing long clothing, and performing tick checks after outdoor activities.
Article_Structure: The article reports that tick season is starting early and intensely, raising concerns among doctors about a potential increase in tick-borne diseases. The main points include: 1) early spike in tick bites according to CDC data, 2) CDC issued an early advisory, 3) limitations of current data (only ER visits), 4) need for further research. The purpose is to inform the public about the elevated risk and encourage preventive actions. Evidence includes CDC surveillance data and expert quotes, but the data is limited. Author credibility: Mike Stobbe is an Associated Press journalist covering health. Methodology: The article relies on CDC tracking data and interviews with experts.

perspectives.description: Doctors like Dr. Filozov warn that more tick bites could lead to more diseases.
The CDC emphasizes early action based on data showing increased ER visits.
